stem: A automotive testing study measures the fuel consumption (L/100km) for a sample of 25 vehicles. The sample mean is 393.0 L/100km with a sample standard deviation of 119.5 L/100km. However, 3 of the 25 observations appear to be outliers (values more than 2.5 standard deviations from the mean). If these outliers are removed, the remaining 22 observations have a mean of 373.1 L/100km and standard deviation of 87.5 L/100km. Estimate the true population mean and provide a 95% confidence interval that accounts for the uncertainty about whether outliers should be included.

ground_truth.answer: With all data: mean = 393.0, 95% CI = [343.7, 442.3]. Without outliers: mean = 373.1, 95% CI = [335.8, 410.4]. Combined interval accounting for analytic uncertainty: [335.8, 442.3]. Point estimate: 383.1 L/100km.

solution_steps: The outlier treatment choice creates structural uncertainty. Combined interval: [335.8, 442.3].
common_failure_modes: Using only the full sample without considering outlier impact
Removing outliers without reporting both analyses
Not recognizing that the choice of analysis method is itself a source of uncertainty
